title: What Happens Without Tests
intro: You're working on a growing e-commerce app with zero tests. It's like driving without mirrors -- you can't see what your changes might hit. The team keeps shipping features fast, but lately things keep breaking. A simple CSS change just broke the entire checkout flow. Experience firsthand what happens when you skip testing.

teach.title: No Tests = Driving Without Mirrors
teach.body: Driving without side mirrors seems fine... until you need to change lanes. You have no idea what's behind you or beside you. Without tests, you can't see what your code changes might hit. Every change is a lane change with no mirrors -- you might be fine, or you might crash into something you didn't even know was there.

teach.title: No Tests = Removing Guardrails from a Mountain Road
teach.body: Mountain roads have guardrails for a reason. On a clear day, you might think they're unnecessary -- you can see the road just fine. But at night, in fog, or when you're tired, those guardrails are the only thing between you and a cliff. **Skipping tests** is like removing the guardrails. Things seem fine when conditions are perfect, but the moment complexity creeps in, there's nothing to stop you from going over the edge.

teach.title: The Slow Decay of an Untested Codebase
teach.body:
At first, no tests seems fine -- the app is small and you know every line. But as it grows:

1. **Week 1**: Small app, you remember everything. No bugs.
2. **Month 3**: App is bigger. Bugs slip in occasionally.
3. **Month 6**: Nobody remembers how old code works. Changes break things.
4. **Year 1**: Everyone is afraid to touch anything. New features take 3x longer because of unexpected breakage.

teach.title: The Fear Factor
teach.body: The worst consequence of no tests is **fear**. Developers become afraid to change code because they don't know what will break. Instead of cleaning up messy code, they add workarounds on top. Instead of refactoring, they copy-paste. The codebase gets worse and worse because nobody dares to improve it.

An app without tests is an app you're afraid to update. And an app you can't update is an app that dies. If you want your vibecoded project to keep earning money, tests are what let you ship new features without breaking the ones your users already pay for.
Without tests, codebases slowly decay. Developers become afraid to change code, bugs slip through to production, and every new feature risks breaking old ones.

start.narrative: Your e-commerce app has 50 pages and zero tests. You need to update the currency formatting function to support euros. It's used in dozens of places: product pages, cart, checkout, receipts, and admin reports. How do you approach this?

choices.text: Change the function and manually click every page that uses it
Write tests for the formatter first, then change it (test-first)
Create a new euro-only function alongside the old dollar one

manual-check.narrative: You spend 2 hours clicking through the app. Product pages look fine. Cart looks fine. You deploy. Monday morning, the admin report (which you forgot to check) is showing prices as NaN. Finance has been exporting broken reports all weekend.

choices.text: Fix the admin report and pin a regression test for the formatter
Fix the admin report and move on -- you spotted it now

write-tests-first.narrative: You write 8 tests for the formatter: dollars, euros, zero, negative, large numbers, missing currency, null, and the admin report format. Then you change the function. 6 tests pass, 2 fail -- admin report format and null handling broke. You fix them in 5 minutes.

choices.text: All tests green -- now plan how to keep coverage up across the codebase
Tests pass -- ship it, deal with the rest of the codebase later

duplicate.narrative: Now you have two formatting functions doing almost the same thing. Some pages use the old one, some the new one. In 3 months, someone changes one and forgets the other. This is exactly how untested codebases decay -- duplication grows because no one trusts the original.

choices.text: Delete the duplicate, write tests for the original, and modify it properly
Leave both for now -- it's not actively breaking anything

audit-coverage.narrative: The formatter is fixed. But the codebase still has zero tests anywhere else. The next breakage is just waiting for the next change. You have one afternoon to invest in coverage. Where do you spend it?

choices.text: Cover the highest-risk modules first: checkout, payments, currency, auth
Aim for 100% coverage of the smallest, easiest utility files
Add a single end-to-end test that clicks through every page

wire-ci.narrative: You've got tests for the riskiest paths. They're useless if no one runs them. How do you make sure they actually catch regressions before users do?

choices.text: Run tests in CI on every pull request and block merges on failures
Tell the team to run tests locally before pushing -- trust the process
Run tests nightly and email failures to the team channel

decide-ship.narrative: CI is wired up. Tests pass. The team is asking when you'll deploy the currency change. What's the responsible final move?

choices.text: Deploy behind a feature flag, watch error rates, then roll out widely
Deploy straight to production -- the tests are green
Wait two weeks for more manual QA before any deploy

end-good.narrative: You ship behind a flag. A small percentage of users hit the new formatter while you watch error logs. Zero anomalies, so you ramp to 100% the next day. CI now blocks any future change that breaks currency formatting -- and the rest of the high-risk modules are covered too.
end-good.feedback: Textbook turnaround: write tests first, prioritize risky modules, wire CI, and ship behind a flag. The codebase is no longer one CSS change away from disaster. Every future contributor inherits a safety net you built in a single afternoon.

end-okay.narrative: The change ships. Tests pass, no immediate fires. But you skipped the gradual rollout, so if something subtle were wrong, every user would have hit it at once. You got lucky this time.
end-okay.feedback: You did the hard work -- tests, CI, prioritization. Skipping the feature-flag rollout was the only weak link. Tests catch known failure modes; staged rollouts catch the unknowns. Use both.

end-poor.narrative: Two weeks of manual QA later, you finally deploy. The team is frustrated by the delay, and the manual QA still missed an edge case in the admin report. Your hard-won tests are sitting unused while you re-click pages by hand.
end-poor.feedback: Tests exist to replace manual checking, not supplement it indefinitely. If you trust your test suite enough to write it, trust it enough to ship behind it. Otherwise the tests become decoration.
